docs.title: Attention Is All You Need
docs.text:
**Authors:** Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Lukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin **Year:** 2017 **Venue:** NeurIPS **Paper ID:** 204e3073870fae3d05bcbc2f6a8e263d9b72e776

[!NOTE] This page is being generated in a high-quality Wikipedia style.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The dominant sequence transduction models have historically been based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks. This paper proposes the Transformer, a novel network architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. The model proves to be highly parallelizable, significantly faster to train, and achieves state-of-the-art results on machine translation tasks.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The core of the Transformer architecture is its reliance on the Self-Attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output. The most critical component introduced is Scaled Dot-Product Attention, defined as:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \]

To allow the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions, the authors introduce Multi-Head Attention:

$$ \text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, ..., \text{head}_h)W^O $$ where \(\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)\).

The architecture features an encoder-decoder structure. Since it lacks recurrence or convolutions, Positional Encodings (using sine and cosine functions of different frequencies) are injected into the input embeddings to provide information about the relative or absolute position of tokens in the sequence. The Transformer achieves significant improvements over RNN-based encoder-decoder architectures, requiring far fewer sequential operations and exhibiting a shorter maximum path length for learning long-range dependencies.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition Transformer A sequence transduction model based entirely on attention mechanisms, avoiding recurrent layers. Self-Attention An attention mechanism relating different positions of a single sequence to compute its representation. Scaled Dot-Product Attention An attention function that scales the dot product of queries and keys by the inverse square root of the key dimension to prevent small gradients in the softmax. Multi-Head Attention A mechanism that runs several attention layers in parallel, allowing the model to attend to different representation subspaces. Positional Encoding Fixed or learned representations added to input embeddings to inject sequence order information into models lacking recurrence.

docs.text:

  • Convolutional Sequence to Sequence Learning
  • Massive Exploration of Neural Machine Translation Architectures
  • A Structured Self-attentive Sentence Embedding

docs.title: LeWorldModel (LeWM)
docs.text:
**Authors:** Lucas Maes, Quentin Le Lidec, Damien Scieur, Yann LeCun, Randall Balestriero **Year:** 2026 **Venue:** N/A **Paper ID:** 530dab86cb8034bc12a32d21508aaa3f2cc00aa1

[!NOTE] This page is being generated in a high-quality Wikipedia style.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: LeWorldModel (LeWM) introduces a stable end-to-end method for learning latent world models from raw pixels using a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA). Traditional JEPA approaches for world modeling suffer from instability and representation collapse, often requiring complex multi-term losses or frozen pre-trained encoders. LeWM overcomes this by employing a simple two-term objective: a next-embedding prediction loss and a Sketched-Isotropic-Gaussian Regularizer (SIGReg) that forces the latent embeddings to match a Gaussian distribution. This dramatically simplifies hyperparameter tuning and enables fast, efficient latent planning for complex continuous control tasks.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The LeWM framework relies on two primary components: an encoder and a predictor. The encoder maps a raw pixel observation \(o_t\) into a compact latent representation \(z_t\). The predictor models the environment dynamics autoregressively by predicting the next frame's embedding \(\hat{z}_{t+1}\) conditioned on the current state \(z_t\) and an action \(a_t\): $$ z_t = \text{enc}{\theta}(o_t) $$ $$ \hat{z}(z_t, a_t) $$} = \text{pred}_{\phi

The architecture is trained end-to-end using a remarkably streamlined objective function: $$ \mathcal{L}{\text{LeWM}} \triangleq \mathcal{L}) $$}} + \lambda \text{SIGReg}(\mathbf{Z

Here, \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{pred}} \triangleq \|\hat{z}_{t+1} - z_{t+1}\|_2^2\) is the mean-squared error for the dynamics prediction. To prevent the trivial representation collapse typical of JEPA architectures, LeWM introduces SIGReg. Because measuring normality in high dimensions is challenging, SIGReg projects the batch of embeddings \(\mathbf{Z}\) onto \(M\) random 1D directions \(u^{(m)} \in \mathbb{S}^{D-1}\) and applies a univariate normality test, the Epps-Pulley test \(T(h^{(m)})\): $$ \text{SIGReg}(\mathbf{Z}) \triangleq \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^M T(h^{(m)}) $$

This optimization encourages the full joint distribution to be isotropic Gaussian, naturally preventing collapse. Once trained, LeWM acts as a simulator, allowing agents to perform Model Predictive Control (MPC) using the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) to optimize sequences of actions entirely within the latent space.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition World Model A predictive model of an environment's dynamics, allowing an agent to simulate future states in a latent imagination space without executing them in reality. Representation Collapse A trivial solution where an encoder maps all distinct inputs to the exact same point or subspace, effectively rendering the representations useless for prediction or discrimination. Sketched-Isotropic-Gaussian Regularizer (SIGReg) A regularization technique that projects high-dimensional embeddings onto random 1D lines and enforces normality, effectively steering the multi-dimensional distribution towards an isotropic Gaussian. Latent Planning The process of optimizing action sequences by rolling out future trajectories in the low-dimensional space of a world model rather than pixel space.

  • A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence Version 0.9.2, 2022-06-27

  • LeJEPA: Provable and Scalable Self-Supervised Learning Without the Heuristics

docs.title: TurboQuant: Online Vector Quantization
docs.text:
**Authors:** Amir Zandieh, Majid Daliri, Majid Hadian, Vahab Mirrokni **Year:** 2025 **Venue:** arXiv **Paper ID:** 65780e86fa36e354da618499f8b4616ac87838bf

[!NOTE] This page is being generated in a high-quality Wikipedia style.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Vector quantization aims to compress high-dimensional Euclidean vectors into low-bitwidth integers while minimizing structural distortion, a core operation for tasks such as KV cache compression in LLMs and nearest neighbor search. Existing methods either lack online applicability or exhibit suboptimal distortion bounds. TurboQuant solves this by presenting a fast, data-oblivious, online vector quantization algorithm that achieves near-optimal distortion rates. It mitigates both Mean-Squared Error (MSE) and inner product distortion, operating efficiently without the heavy preprocessing steps characterizing traditional product quantization techniques.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
TurboQuant operates on worst-case vectors (normalized to the unit sphere) and fundamentally reshapes their distribution by applying a random rotation matrix \(\mathbf{\Pi} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}\). This random projection induces a Beta distribution (which approaches a Gaussian in high dimensions) on every coordinate of \(\mathbf{\Pi}\mathbf{x}\), rendering distinct coordinates virtually independent. This allows TurboQuant to apply an optimal 1D continuous Lloyd-Max scalar quantizer independently per coordinate.

For a bit-width \(b\), the MSE-optimal quantizer \(Q_{mse}\) guarantees a bounded expected distortion tightly matching the theoretical Shannon Lower Bound. Specifically, the distortion is bound by: $$ D_{mse}(Q_{mse}) := \mathbb{E} \left[ |\mathbf{x} - Q_{mse}^{-1}(Q_{mse}(\mathbf{x}))|_2^2 \right] \le \frac{\sqrt{3}\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4^b} $$

However, MSE-optimal quantizers are biased for inner product estimations. To correct this, the paper introduces a two-stage approach for inner product quantization (\(Q_{prod}\)): 1. Quantize the rotated vector using an MSE-optimized scalar quantizer at bit-width \(b-1\). 2. Apply a 1-bit Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss (QJL) transform on the remaining residual vector \(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{x} - \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{mse}\).

The reconstructed vector yields an unbiased inner product estimator: $$ \mathbb{E}\left[\langle \mathbf{y}, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}{mse} + \mathbf{r} \rangle $$ This method dramatically improves performance in LLM long-context tasks, allowing KV cache compression up to } \rangle\right] = \langle \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x\(4.5\times\) (approx. 2.5-3.5 bits per float) without degradation, while massively speeding up inner product approximations for database indexing.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition Vector Quantization The process of mapping a continuous or high-precision multi-dimensional space into a finite set of discrete values or low-precision integers to save memory. Mean-Squared Error (MSE) Distortion A metric capturing the expected squared Euclidean distance between original and quantized vectors. Shannon Lower Bound (SLB) A fundamental information-theoretic lower bound on the optimal achievable distortion rate for any lossy compression scheme. Data-Oblivious An algorithm that functions universally without relying on specific statistics or characteristics of the underlying dataset, making it ideal for online settings. Product Quantization (PQ) A classic nearest-neighbor approach dividing vectors into sub-vectors and quantizing them separately, usually heavily dependent on offline training (k-means).

  • QJL: 1-Bit Quantized JL Transform for KV Cache Quantization with Zero Overhead
  • Polarquant: Quantizing kv caches with polar transformation

docs.title: QJL: 1-Bit Quantized JL Transform
docs.text:
**Authors:** Amir Zandieh, Majid Daliri, Insu Han **Year:** 2024 **Venue:** AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence **Paper ID:** 7318a804566baadc9f4b4ca8255f78744e749a32

[!NOTE] This page is being generated in a high-quality Wikipedia style.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) requires substantial memory due to the storage requirements of Key-Value (KV) embeddings in the KV cache, which grows with sequence length. This paper introduces QJL, a quantization approach combining a Johnson-Lindenstrauss (JL) transform followed by sign-bit quantization. Unlike existing techniques, QJL eliminates memory overheads by removing the need for storing quantization constants, allowing the KV cache to be aggressively compressed while preserving model accuracy and enabling high-throughput inference for long contexts.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The QJL (Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss) method tackles the substantial memory consumption of the KV cache during the token generation decoding phase of auto-regressive transformers. The core idea is to apply a 1-bit quantization mapping to vectors using a random projection matrix \(\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}\):

\[ \mathcal{H}_S(\mathbf{k}) := \text{sign}(\mathbf{S} \cdot \mathbf{k}) \]

To achieve an unbiased estimate of the inner product of two vectors (crucial for computing attention scores \(\text{softmax}(\mathbf{q}^T \mathbf{k})\) without amplifying error), QJL uses an asymmetric estimator where only the key is quantized to a single bit while the query remains unquantized:

\[ \text{Prod}_{QJL}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}) := \frac{\sqrt{\pi/2}}{m} \|\mathbf{k}\|_2 \cdot \langle \mathbf{S}\mathbf{q}, \mathcal{H}_S(\mathbf{k}) \rangle \]

The authors provide theoretical guarantees that \(\text{Prod}_{QJL}\) is an unbiased estimator with bounded distortion. This method is data-oblivious, does not require fine-tuning, and uses an orthogonalized JL transform to improve empirical performance. It successfully compresses the KV cache to only 3 bits per floating-point number with virtually zero accuracy loss.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition KV Cache Memory used in auto-regressive transformers to store previously generated key and value embeddings, preventing redundant recomputations. Johnson-Lindenstrauss (JL) Transform A mathematical technique involving a random projection that preserves the distances and inner products between points in a high-dimensional space. QJL Transform A mapping that projects a vector using a JL transform and then quantizes the result to its sign bit (1-bit). Asymmetric Estimator An inner product estimation technique where one vector is fully quantized while the other is kept unquantized, ensuring unbiased and low-distortion results.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Attention is All you Need
  • Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need
  • High-throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU
  • KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache
  • KVQuant: Towards 10 Million Context Length LLM Inference with KV Cache Quantization

docs.title: Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (I-JEPA)
docs.text:
**Authors:** Mahmoud Assran, Quentin Duval, Ishan Misra, Piotr Bojanowski, Pascal Vincent, Michael Rabbat, Yann LeCun, Nicolas Ballas **Year:** 2023 **Venue:** CVPR **Paper ID:** ee57e4d7a125f4ca8916284a857c3760d7d378d3

[!NOTE] This page is being generated in a high-quality Wikipedia style.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This paper introduces the Image-based Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (I-JEPA), an approach for learning highly semantic image representations without relying on hand-crafted data augmentations. I-JEPA is a non-generative self-supervised learning technique where the model learns by predicting the representations of various target blocks in an image given a single context block. By predicting in representation space instead of pixel space, I-JEPA naturally avoids capturing unnecessary low-level details, yielding representations that are more semantic and an architecture that scales efficiently.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The I-JEPA architecture comprises three main components: a context encoder, a target encoder, and a predictor network, all based on Vision Transformers (ViTs). Unlike generative methods such as Masked Autoencoders (MAE) that aim to reconstruct pixel-level targets, I-JEPA predicts abstract representations. Given an input image, a context block \(x\) is fed through the context encoder to obtain representations \(s_x\). The target encoder processes the full image to generate representations \(s_y\), which are then extracted for specific target blocks \(B_i\).

The predictor network \(g_{\phi}\) takes the context representations \(s_x\) and positional mask tokens corresponding to the target blocks to predict the target representations: $$ \hat{s}y(i) = g) $$}(s_x, {m_j}_{j \in B_i

The model is optimized using an average \(L_2\) distance loss between the predicted patch-level representation and the target patch-level representation: $$ \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M D(\hat{s}y(i), s_y(i)) = \frac{1}{M}\sum}^M \sum_{j \in B_i} |\hat{s{y_j} - s|_2^2 $$

To prevent representation collapse, I-JEPA relies on an asymmetric design where the target encoder's weights are updated as an exponential moving average (EMA) of the context encoder's weights. A critical design choice is the multi-block masking strategy, which ensures the target blocks are large enough to contain semantic information and the context is informative but sparse.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) A self-supervised framework where a predictor network learns to map representations of a context signal to the representations of target signals in an abstract embedding space. Representation Collapse A failure mode in joint-embedding architectures where the encoder ignores the input and outputs a constant vector, artificially minimizing the distance between pairs. Exponential Moving Average (EMA) An update mechanism where the weights of a target network smoothly track the weights of the online network over time, preventing collapse in self-supervised learning. Multi-Block Masking A masking strategy that samples multiple, potentially overlapping target blocks and a single, spatially disjoint context block to force the model to learn semantic dependencies rather than trivial local continuities.

  • Masked autoencoders are scalable vision learners
  • Emerging properties in self-supervised vision transformers
  • Data2vec: A general framework for self-supervised learning in speech, vision and language

docs.title: PolarQuant: Quantizing KV Caches with Polar Transformation
docs.text:
**Authors:** Insu Han, Praneeth Kacham, Amin Karbasi, Vahab Mirrokni, Amir Zandieh **Year:** 2025 **Venue:** arXiv (cs.LG) **Paper ID:** ef9485a2522f64bca0f5cf67edc28a11984790e8

PolarQuant is a specialized quantization framework designed to compress the Key-Value (KV) cache in Large Language Models (LLMs). By representing embedding vectors in polar coordinates rather than traditional Cartesian coordinates, PolarQuant achieves high compression ratios (up to 4.2x) with minimal loss in model accuracy. The method leverages random preconditioning to induce a predictable distribution of angles, eliminating the need for expensive per-block normalization.

docs.title: The KV Cache Bottleneck
docs.text: In autoregressive Transformers, the KV cache stores key and value embeddings for all previously generated tokens to avoid redundant computations. As context length increases, the memory demand for these caches scales linearly, often becoming the primary bottleneck for serving long-sequence models.

docs.title: Polar Transformation Heuristic
docs.text:
Traditional quantization methods (like 4-bit or 8-bit integer quantization) operate on Cartesian coordinates and require storing scale and zero-point metadata for each block of numbers. PolarQuant shifts this paradigm by converting vectors into a Recursive Polar Transformation.

For a vector \(x \in \mathbb{R}^d\), the transformation groups coordinates into pairs and recursively computes radii and angles: $\(\psi^{(1)}_j = \tan^{-1}(x_{2j}/x_{2j-1})\)$ $\(r^{(1)}_j = \sqrt{x_{2j-1}^2 + x_{2j}^2}\)$

This process is repeated \(\log_2 d\) times, resulting in a single final radius and a set of angles.

docs.title: Random Preconditioning
docs.text:
A core contribution of PolarQuant is the use of Random Preconditioning (multiplying the input by a random rotation matrix \(S\)). According to the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma, this preserves the inner product while ensuring the resulting vectors follow a multivariate normal distribution.

Crucially, after preconditioning, the angles \(\psi\) exhibit a tightly bounded and highly concentrated distribution (Lemma 1). This concentration allows for the design of optimal, data-oblivious codebooks, removing the overhead of storing per-block normalization constants.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition KV Cache A memory buffer used in Transformers to store Key and Value vectors of previous tokens to accelerate inference. Quantization The process of mapping continuous or high-precision values to a finite set of discrete symbols to reduce memory. Random Preconditioning Applying a random rotation matrix to input data to normalize its distribution while preserving geometric properties. Polar Coordinates A 2D coordinate system where each point is determined by a distance from a reference point and an angle from a reference direction. Data-Oblivious Algorithms that do not require access to the specific input distribution to maintain performance.
